behaviors.description: Out-of-stock products excluded from recommendations regardless of relevance score
behaviors.justification: A unit test could verify the filter logic in isolation, but the real failure would be integration-level: the availability field might not be queried from the product catalog correctly, the database join might be missing, or the schema might differ from expectations. A mock product list would always pass; real data with missing/inconsistent availability fields would catch the gap.
behaviors.test_description: Test the full recommendation pipeline with a test database containing both in-stock and out-of-stock products at various relevance scores. Verify that no out-of-stock products appear in results, even if they score high. Repeat with different catalog states to ensure the filter is applied consistently.

behaviors.description: User preference filter correctly excludes blocked product categories
behaviors.justification: Unit test could verify the filter logic, but real failure would occur at integration boundaries: user settings might not load correctly, category names in the taxonomy might not match blocked category strings exactly, or category hierarchies might not be flattened properly. Mocking perfect category data masks these issues.
behaviors.test_description: Create test user with explicitly blocked categories in settings. Test against a real (test) database with products in those categories. Verify blocked products don't appear in recommendations. Test with various category name formats (case sensitivity, synonyms, hierarchical paths) to ensure robust matching.

behaviors.description: Products matching recent browsing patterns rank higher than products without browsing signal
behaviors.justification: Unit test could verify the scoring formula weights browsing history correctly, but real failure would be at integration level: browsing history query might be incomplete, time-window cutoffs might be wrong, or the scoring formula might not be applied correctly in the context of real data. Mock data with perfect browsing history masks these issues.
behaviors.test_description: Load real (test) user browsing history with known patterns. Run recommendations and verify products matching recent browsing patterns appear in higher positions than unrelated products with similar popularity scores. Test with various browsing history depths and recency to ensure the weighting algorithm works correctly.

behaviors.description: Pagination returns correct page with stable ordering—requesting page 2 twice returns identical results
behaviors.justification: Unit test could verify offset/limit math, but real failures happen at the database level: ORDER BY clause might be missing, ordering might be by a non-unique field, or database behavior might vary across implementations. Mock in-memory pagination always works correctly; real database queries might not have stable ordering.
behaviors.test_description: Test pagination against a real (test) database with sufficient products. Request the same page multiple times and verify results are bit-for-bit identical. Verify ordering is deterministic by checking that products appear in the same order across requests. Test boundary conditions (first page, last page, off-by-one cases).

behaviors.description: Serendipity requirement—at least one product from category user hasn't browsed before
behaviors.justification: Unit test could verify the serendipity branching logic ('if unbrowsed categories exist, pick one'), but real failure would be at integration level: category mapping might be ambiguous, browsing history query might be incomplete, or the unbrowsed-category calculation might fail with real data. Mock with clean category data masks these issues.
behaviors.test_description: Create test user with browsing history covering only some categories. Run recommendations and verify at least one result comes from a category not in the browsing history. Test with various category counts and browsing patterns. Verify edge cases: user who has browsed all categories (fallback behavior), user with no browsing history (all categories unbrowsed).

behaviors.description: New user with no purchase/browsing history receives popularity-based recommendations instead of empty results
behaviors.justification: Unit test could verify the fallback logic ('if no history, use popularity'), but real failures happen at integration level: popularity calculation might be wrong with real data, the fallback might not trigger due to stale/partial history in the database, or the popularity scores might not be populated correctly. Mock with clean zero-history scenarios masks these issues.
behaviors.test_description: Create a new user account with zero purchase and browsing history. Request recommendations and verify results are returned (not empty). Verify results are ranked by popularity (higher-popularity products appear first). Verify the fallback logic is actually triggered by examining debug logs or internal state.

behaviors.description: Click tracking records correct product ID, position in list, and timestamp for each recommendation
behaviors.justification: Unit test could verify the click event object is constructed correctly, but real failures occur at integration level: events might not be persisted to the tracking database, timestamps might be in the wrong timezone or have precision loss, position might be off-by-one, or data might be corrupted in transit. Mock in-memory event objects mask persistence failures.
behaviors.test_description: Simulate user clicks on recommendations at various positions. Query the real (test) click tracking database and verify each click is recorded with correct product_id, position, and timestamp. Verify timestamps are consistent and precise (no timezone issues). Test rapid clicks to ensure no loss of events. Verify position matches the original result position.

behaviors.description: Response time stays under 200ms for catalog of 100,000 products
behaviors.justification: This is a performance/load requirement that cannot be tested with mocks or small datasets. Unit and integration tests with dozens of products will always pass (mocks are fast by definition), but real data at scale reveals bottlenecks: missing database indexes, N+1 queries, memory issues, or algorithmic inefficiencies. Only real infrastructure with real scale catches this failure.
behaviors.test_description: Populate a real (test) database with 100,000 products with realistic distribution across categories, prices, and availability. Measure recommendation request latency across multiple requests and various user profiles (new users, power users, etc.). Verify 95th percentile latency stays under 200ms. Profile queries and algorithm to identify bottlenecks if threshold is breached.

behaviors.description: When recommended product is purchased, algorithm deprioritizes it in subsequent requests
behaviors.justification: Unit test could verify the deprioritization filter logic, but real failures occur at integration level: purchase history might not be loaded from the database, the deprioritization logic might not be applied in the actual ranking pipeline, or there might be a race condition where the purchase is recorded but not visible immediately. Mock with known purchase history masks these issues.
behaviors.test_description: Create test user, get recommendations, simulate purchase of a recommended product, then request recommendations again. Verify the purchased product appears lower in rankings (or doesn't appear if fully deprioritized). Verify with multiple purchases to ensure the deprioritization logic is cumulative. Check that other products maintain their relative ranking.

behaviors.description: Recommendation results are deterministic for same user state—identical requests return same ranked list
behaviors.justification: Unit test could verify a single scoring function is deterministic, but real failures occur at integration level: the full ranking pipeline might not be deterministic due to random sampling/shuffling, floating-point precision issues in scoring comparisons, or database query result ordering. Only integration-level testing of the full pipeline reveals these issues.
behaviors.test_description: Run recommendations for a user twice without any intervening user activity (no purchases, no browsing). Verify the full ranked result set is identical both times: same products in same order. Repeat multiple times across different user profiles. Verify no randomness is introduced by any step in the pipeline (sampling, shuffling, floating-point rounding).
